assertion_text: MARK4 kinase may relay NLRP3 inflammasome activation to tau phosphorylation, as STRING-DB shows MARK4 interacts with both NLRP3 (score 0.645) and MAPT (score 0.659). Butyrate (gut microbiota metabolite) modulation of this MARK4 relay has zero prior publications.
Senescent cells and their senescence-associated secretory phenotypes (SASPs) contribute to neuro-inflammation and subsequent neurodegeneration.
The HIV-1 protein Nef impairs cellular cholesterol homeostasis and lipid rafts, contributing to the pathogenesis of HAND.
Antrodia camphorata has the potential to regulate the gut-microbiome-brain axis to influence cerebral neuropathology.
Alterations in gut microbiota composition contribute to increased amyloid-β aggregation, neuroinflammation, and insulin resistance in the brain.
The therapeutic mechanism of HLJDD in Alzheimer's disease is predicted to involve the regulation of VEGF, NF-κB, and Ca2+ signaling pathways.
Long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) participate in the regulation of neuroinflammatory and immunological pathways consequential to cerebrovascular abnormalities in VCI.
Ferroptosis is a primary underlying mechanism driving accelerated brain aging and neurodegeneration in survivors of COVID-19.

Ketone bodies (acetoacetate and β-hydroxybutyrate) are metabolized into acetyl-CoA in extrahepatic tissues to enter the tricarboxylic acid cycle and electron transport chain for ATP production during glucose deficiency.
HSP60 maintains mitochondrial proteome integrity and homeostasis by facilitating the folding of native proteins.
The specific mechanisms by which gut microbiota mediate lipid metabolism in Alzheimer's disease pathology are currently not fully understood.
The specific molecular mechanisms underlying the interactions between environmental risk factors and microglial phenotypes in Alzheimer's disease remain largely unknown.
Mutations in the α-Syn gene lead to the expression of pathological α-Syn protein which can cause Parkinson's disease.
A 60% kcal high-fat diet (HFD) induces significant increases in body weight and impairs glucose tolerance in wild-type mice compared to regular chow controls.
The non-parenchymal tissues of the CNS, including the meninges, perivascular space, and choroid plexus, are populated by resident innate immune cells.
Mutations in amyloid precursor protein (APP), presenilin-1 (PSEN1), and presenilin-2 (PSEN2) are linked to familial Alzheimer's disease.
Oral nCUR treatment enhances hippocampal neurogenesis and normalizes synaptophysin puncta expression in a chronic rat model of GWI.
HSV-1 proteins ICP34.5 and US11 inhibit PRKN-dependent mitophagy by deregulating the EIF2S1-ATF4 axis to suppress PRKN mRNA expression.
Inflammaging and cellular senescence contribute to the development of chronic kidney disease (CKD) and impaired renal regeneration.
Microglia and astrocytes initiate neuroinflammation and toxic tau and amyloid-β accumulation in Alzheimer's disease.
The gut microbiota-brain axis regulates the neuro-immune and endocrine systems in the context of Alzheimer's disease.
Perturbation of HSP60 results in the loss of mitochondrial integrity and the activation of apoptosis.
SIRT1, Nrf2, NF-κB, Cox-2, and iNOS/NO· serve as core regulatory molecules within the shared pathways governing both ferroptosis and neuroinflammation.
Gut dysbiosis leads to increased production of oxidized low-density lipoproteins (OX-LDLs), which restrict blood vessels and cause hypertension.
Capsaicin treatment increases serum tryptophan metabolism and decreases lipid metabolism in APP/PS1 mice.
RNA editing events occur in transcripts for the protein-coding gene synaptotagmin 1 in human brain datasets associated with Alzheimer's disease.
Transient Middle Cerebral Artery occlusion (tMCAo) causes a time-dependent increase in miR-223-3p, miR-155-5p, miR-3473, and miR-448-5p expression in the ipsilateral cerebral cortex.
Alteration in gut microbiota composition weakens the intestinal barrier, facilitating the translocation of prions from the gut to the brain.
HMGCR-mediated dysregulation of cholesterol metabolism contributes to the pathogenesis of Alzheimer's disease through the promotion of amyloid-β (Aβ) deposition.
The specific mechanisms driving sex-dependent microglial responses in Alzheimer's Disease are not yet fully understood.
NLRP3 inflammasome activation in gingival macrophages reduces their phagocytic capability and promotes a proinflammatory state, exacerbating periodontitis.
Mitochondrial deterioration during aging is driven by impaired biogenesis, disrupted dynamics, dysregulated stress responses, and defective clearance of damaged mitochondria.
Low zinc concentrations suppress amyloid-beta induced neurotoxicity by selectively precipitating aggregation intermediates in Alzheimer's disease.
Mice lacking gut microbiota exhibit increased blood-CSF barrier permeability associated with disorganized tight junction proteins including ZO-1, OCLN, and CDH1.
TGR5 expression is specifically upregulated in neurons of 5×FAD mouse brains at an early stage and is induced by DCA.

Epigenetic alterations contribute to the aberrant expression of genes involved in neuroinflammation, protein aggregation, and neuronal death in neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's and Parkinson's.
Flavonoids inhibit the formation of protein aggregates and promote their removal in the context of neurodegenerative diseases.
The gut microbiota of 5xFAD mice is characterized by a significant reduction in the abundance of Bifidobacterium, Faecalibaculum, and Desulfomicrobium compared to wild-type mice.
Melatonin treatment reduces the levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α) and inflammatory enzymes (iNOS, COX2) in the hippocampus of sleep-deprived rats.
Translocated lung bacteria induce neuroinflammation characterized by the activation of microglia and astrocytes via bacterial infection-related pathways.
lncRNA profiles and functions can guide the development of targeted immunization strategies to mitigate or prevent vascular cognitive impairment.
GMEVs and their cargo routes represent a viable pathway for the delivery of therapeutic drugs to the CNS.
Inhibition of Cathepsin B (CatB) or Cathepsin S (CatS) serves as a therapeutic approach to delay the onset of Alzheimer's disease by dampening inflammation.
Restoring normal cholesterol metabolism in the liver is a potential therapeutic strategy for treating Alzheimer's disease.
Natural TRPV1 agonists represent a promising future treatment strategy for chronic neurodegenerative diseases.
Crude extracts and active metabolites of Antrodia camphorata alleviate neurological symptoms at cellular and molecular levels.
Oral administration of Lactobacillus gasseri MG4247 and Lacticaseibacillus rhamnosus MG4644 significantly improves spatial memory and learning ability in amyloid beta-induced mice.
Current cholinesterase inhibitor medications for Alzheimer's disease are unable to completely arrest disease progression.
Targeting inflammasomes with specific therapies is a viable strategy for developing disease-modifying treatments currently being evaluated in clinical trials.
Reducing blood Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) levels has not yet been proven to reduce Alzheimer's disease risk or progression in humans.
HDAC inhibitors show promise in ameliorating symptoms of neurological diseases, suggesting potential for treating Alzheimer's disease.

Conventional antioxidant and anti-inflammatory therapies, including NSAIDs, corticosteroids, and dietary antioxidants, exhibit limited and inconsistent long-term clinical efficacy due to issues with safety, bioavailability, and efficacy.
Gasdermin D (GSDMD) knockout inhibits astrocyte pyroptosis and reduces neuroinflammation and vascular damage in APP/PS1 mice.
Lingonberries and their insoluble fraction provide a neuroprotective effect on altered synaptic plasticity in ApoE-/- mice.
Naringin treatment can alleviate the toxic side effects associated with other pharmacological agents.
4-octyl itaconate (OI) treatment significantly alleviates anesthesia/surgery-induced cognitive impairment in aged mice.
PEA treatment reduces the accumulation of Aβ and α-synuclein in the colon of SAMP8 mice.
Specific antidiabetic drugs decrease the risk of Parkinson's disease and delay its clinical progression.
Oral administration of Mitoapocynin (MA) at 3 mg/kg attenuates ODE-induced neuroinflammation and reverses sensory-motor deficits.
JDYZF modulates specific gut microbial taxa in AD rats, notably increasing the abundance of Lactobacillus and decreasing Helicobacter.

Oral administration of Kampo formulas (kososan [KS], nobiletin-rich kososan [NKS], and hachimijiogan [HJG]) for 13 weeks significantly reduces depression-like behavior in SAMP8 mice as measured by the tail suspension test.
Oral administration of PEA (5 mg/kg/day) significantly improves in vitro colonic longitudinal muscle contractile activity in SAMP8 mice.
Oleocanthal significantly inhibits the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines and reduces the expression of inflammatory genes in LPS-activated BV-2 microglial cells.
Puerarin demonstrates neuroprotective functions in the treatment of Alzheimer’s disease.
Sodium oligomannate (GV-971) binds directly to the GIANLDKL repeats of the Rib adhesin to disrupt bacterial adherence and alleviate Th1-skewed inflammation.
A combination of inflammatory factors (IL-6, INF-γ), phosphatidylcholines (PCs), and SCFA-producing bacteria serve as potential early diagnostic biomarkers for Alzheimer's disease.

A set of ten genes, including eight up-regulated (SLC22A2, ITGB2-AS1, NIT1, FGF14-AS2, SEMA3E, PYCARD, PRORY, ADIRF) and two down-regulated (AKAIN1, TRMT2B), are differentially expressed in the entorhinal cortex and serve as potential diagnostic targets for AD.
Changes in butyrate levels serve as an early warning indicator for colon cancer.
Brain glucose metabolism reduction in Alzheimer's disease occurs presymptomatically and follows a progressive, region-specific pattern.
Children exposed to high air pollution exhibit significantly higher concentrations of Nickel and antibodies to Myelin Basic Protein (MBP) in their cerebrospinal fluid (CSF).
[18F]ROStrace retention is significantly elevated in the cortex, striatum, and midbrain of 3-month-old MitoPark mice compared to wild-type controls.
PYCARD is significantly up-regulated in the entorhinal cortex of Alzheimer's disease patients and demonstrates high diagnostic accuracy with an AUC of 0.95.
[18F]ROStrace PET imaging identifies increased whole-brain superoxide levels in MitoPark mice as early as 2 months of age, during the prodromal phase of Parkinson's-like pathology.
Brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) levels serve as a biomarker for the inflammatory processes involved in late-life depression.

Children residing in high-pollution areas (MCMA) exhibit significantly elevated serum levels of antibodies (IgG, IgA, IgM) against tight junction proteins (occludin, zonulin 1) and neural proteins (actin, MOG, MBP, S-100).
The oral microbiome is a potentially modifiable risk factor in the development of Alzheimer's disease.
Fibroblast growth factor basic (FGF-basic) is associated with an increased risk of developing vascular dementia (VaD).
Elevated serum homocysteine (Hcy) levels are associated with an increased risk of Alzheimer's disease and Parkinson's disease compared to healthy controls.
Apolipoprotein-E ε4 (APOE ε4) is the strongest common genetic risk allele for Alzheimer's disease.
Serum water-soluble vitamin levels are positively correlated with neuropsychological assessment scores (MMSE, MoCA, BNT) and negatively correlated with dementia severity scales (CDR, UPDRS).
The APOE4 allele is geographically concentrated in regions with high parasitic burden and provides protection against childhood tropical infections and hepatitis C.
Serum levels of palmitoyl-L-carnitine (C16:0) and related acylcarnitines increase significantly with age in mice.
Periodontitis and Alzheimer's disease share common risk factors, including advanced age, smoking, diabetes, and hypertension.
Lower levels of serum vitamins B2, B6, 5mTHF, and B12 are associated with a higher risk of dementia in patients with Parkinson's disease.
A shift in the gut microbiome to a pro-inflammatory phenotype is associated with the onset and progression of Parkinson's disease.
The utilization of natural products in drug discovery for neurodegenerative disorders is currently limited by unresolved ethical issues.
There is a necessity for additional investigation on glial cells and their specific involvement in neurological illnesses.
NLRP3 inflammasome, TREM2, gut microbiota, and extracellular vesicles (exosomes) are identified as the primary research hotspots and emerging trends in the study of microglia in Alzheimer's disease.
There are significant challenges and future opportunities in establishing acupuncture as a standardized treatment for Alzheimer's disease via gut microbiota modulation.
Polyphenols are characterized by low bioavailability, which limits their therapeutic potential for brain health and cognitive function.
Bacterial outer membrane vesicles from Gram-negative bacteria potentially contribute to Parkinson's disease pathogenesis by traversing the gut lumen to trigger local, systemic, and neuroinflammation.
The specific cellular mechanisms and molecular signatures that facilitate the crosstalk between obesity, T2D, and AD during disease progression remain largely unknown.
Conventional animal-based models are limited in their ability to model the multifactorial and gradual progression of human neurodegenerative diseases.
Peripheral inflammation overwhelms glymphatic system performance, triggering chronic neuroinflammation and neurodegeneration.
Aromatic-turmerone, Apocynin, and Matrine are identified as the most promising neuroprotective candidates for modulating microglial polarization based on computational screening of 54 phytochemicals.
The majority of the 54 screened neuroprotective phytochemicals are predicted to possess anti-inflammatory activity using the PASS online webserver.
HLJDD targets 163 unique proteins associated with Alzheimer's disease, with AKT1, TNF, TP53, VEGFA, FOS, PTGS2, MMP9, and CASP3 identified as core therapeutic targets.
Coarse particulate matter (PM2.5–10) does not have a significant causal association with Alzheimer's Disease.
Natural extracellular vesicles are limited in clinical utility due to poor targeting specificity and rapid systemic clearance.
The Y-maze test fails to show cognitive deficits in APP/PS1 mice or therapeutic response to β-glucan treatment under the study conditions.
